---
title: "Cholesky and the Matrix Square Root"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Geometry of Data"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D  # noqa: F401

# One restrained visual style for every figure in this article, matched to the
# rest of the Geometry-of-Data track.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

VEC    = "#2b3a67"   # generic vectors / data
HILITE = "#c1121f"   # eigen-axes / principal directions / Cholesky factor
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"   # second axis / highlights
GREY   = "#6c757d"
PALE   = "#9fb3d1"


def draw_vec(ax, tail, head, color=VEC, lw=2.4, label=None, z=5,
             label_offset=(0.0, 0.0), ls="-"):
    """Draw a 2-D arrow from tail to head with a clean head."""
    ax.annotate("", xy=head, xytext=tail,
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=color, lw=lw,
                                shrinkA=0, shrinkB=0, linestyle=ls), zorder=z)
    if label is not None:
        ax.text(head[0] + label_offset[0], head[1] + label_offset[1], label,
                color=color, fontsize=13, ha="center", va="center", zorder=z + 1)
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This article pairs with [GEOM-20 *The Singular Value
> Decomposition*] to close **Movement V**. It assumes the **spectral theorem** — that a
> real symmetric matrix factors as $A = Q\Lambda Q^\top$ with $Q$ orthogonal
> ($Q^\top Q = I$) and $\Lambda$ diagonal ([GEOM-16]); **quadratic forms** $q(x)=x^\top A x$
> and their classification by eigenvalue sign ([GEOM-17]); and **positive-definite
> matrices** — the matrices with $x^\top A x > 0$ for all $x \neq 0$, equivalently all
> eigenvalues positive ([GEOM-18]). It also leans on **solving $Ax=b$** and the meaning of
> the inverse ([GEOM-11]), the **transpose** algebra $(AB)^\top = B^\top A^\top$ ([GEOM-10]),
> and the **dot product / norm** $\|x\|^2 = x^\top x$ ([GEOM-04]). The one question: *how do
> we factor a positive-definite matrix into a "square," and why does that single trick power
> simulation, whitening, and fast solves?* It sets up [GEOM-26] (Cholesky as the stable
> positive-definite solve) and **directly discharges Regression's GLS / whitening
> prerequisite** ([REGR-11]) and the **correlated-Gaussian sampling** that Probability and
> Inference lean on.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

A positive-definite matrix $A$ behaves like a positive number: it has a square root.
This article builds *two* of them, for two different jobs. The **Cholesky factorization**
$A = LL^\top$ writes $A$ as a lower-triangular matrix times its own transpose; we derive
$L$ constructively, entry by entry, and prove that the requirement that every entry be
computable from real arithmetic is *exactly* positive-definiteness — so a
Cholesky factor exists if and only if $A$ is positive definite, with no separate
hypothesis. The **symmetric square root** $A^{1/2} = Q\Lambda^{1/2}Q^\top$ comes from the
spectral theorem instead, and is the unique symmetric positive-definite matrix whose
square is $A$. Both are "square roots," and the article's single claim is that **the
choice between them is the choice between speed and symmetry**: Cholesky is the fast,
triangular workhorse for solving and sampling; the symmetric root is the geometrically
honest one for whitening and for anything that must itself be symmetric. We carry three
real payoffs all the way through — **whitening** a correlated dataset ($\Sigma^{-1/2}$,
the engine of generalized least squares), **sampling** a correlated Gaussian ($Lz$), and
**solving** a positive-definite system twice as fast as general elimination — and we mark
the honest edge where positive-definiteness frays and Cholesky quietly fails.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
A positive-definite matrix is the matrix world's version of a positive number, and like a
positive number it has a square root. There are two natural ones.

**Cholesky:** every positive-definite $A$ factors *uniquely* as
$$A = L L^\top, \qquad L \text{ lower-triangular with positive diagonal.}$$
$L$ is built directly from the entries of $A$ by a short recursion, and the recursion only
succeeds — every diagonal entry it needs a square root of comes out positive — *precisely
when $A$ is positive definite*. So "Cholesky exists" and "$A$ is positive definite" are
the same statement.

**Symmetric square root:** from the spectral theorem $A = Q\Lambda Q^\top$, take the
positive square root of each eigenvalue:
$$A^{1/2} = Q\,\Lambda^{1/2}\,Q^\top, \qquad \Lambda^{1/2} = \operatorname{diag}\bigl(\sqrt{\lambda_1},\dots,\sqrt{\lambda_n}\bigr),$$
the unique *symmetric* positive-definite matrix with $A^{1/2}A^{1/2} = A$.

Both satisfy "factor $\times$ its transpose $= A$." Cholesky's factor is **triangular**
(fast to solve with, the natural sampler); the spectral root is **symmetric** (the natural
whitener). One trick — write $A$ as a square — and three payoffs fall out: **sample**
correlated noise with $Lz$, **whiten** correlated data with $\Sigma^{-1/2}$, and **solve**
$Ax=b$ at half the cost of general elimination.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The phrase "matrix square root" will at first sound like
a stretch — matrices are not numbers. By the end it should feel inevitable: a
positive-definite matrix *is* built from positive numbers (its eigenvalues), and taking
their square roots, in the right basis, gives a matrix that squares back to the original
exactly as $\sqrt{a}\cdot\sqrt{a}=a$ does for a positive scalar.

# Why we are doing this: the problem that forced the factorization {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **why would anyone want to write a matrix
as a square, and why does positive-definiteness make it possible?** Every step below is in
service of that.

## A scalar habit we cannot give up

Half of applied mathematics is built on the ability to take a square root of a positive
number. Standardizing a measurement means subtracting the mean and **dividing by the
standard deviation** $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$. Generating a normal random variable with
variance $\sigma^2$ means multiplying a standard normal by $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$.
Both moves are "undo, or impose, a known spread," and both run on the scalar square root.

The instant our data have more than one feature, "spread" stops being a single number
$\sigma^2$ and becomes a **covariance matrix** $\Sigma$ — a symmetric matrix whose diagonal
holds the per-feature variances and whose off-diagonals hold how features move together.
(Its construction as a statistical object belongs to Probability; here it is simply a
symmetric, positive-definite matrix, the canonical one in data science.) And now the two
scalar habits demand a *matrix* square root:

- **Imposing spread (simulation).** To draw a random vector with covariance $\Sigma$, we
  need a matrix $B$ with $BB^\top = \Sigma$, because feeding uncorrelated unit noise $z$
  through $B$ produces $Bz$ with covariance $\Sigma$. $B$ is a "square root" of $\Sigma$.
- **Undoing spread (whitening).** To strip the correlations *out* of data with covariance
  $\Sigma$ — to make every direction equally and unitly scattered, which is what unlocks
  generalized least squares ([REGR-11]) — we need to multiply by $\Sigma^{-1/2}$, the
  inverse of a square root.

There is no way around it: the multivariate versions of the most basic statistical moves
*require* a matrix that, multiplied by (a version of) itself, returns $\Sigma$. The whole
article is the construction and the geometry of such a matrix.

## The third pressure: solving fast, and solving stably

A separate, older pressure comes from solving linear systems. Many systems in
data science have a **positive-definite** coefficient matrix — the normal equations
$X^\top X\,\hat\beta = X^\top y$ of least squares ([REGR-05]), the systems Newton's method
produces from a positive-definite Hessian ([GEOM-24]), the covariance systems of Gaussian
inference. General Gaussian elimination ([GEOM-11]) solves *any* invertible system, but it
"wastes" the extra structure positive-definite matrices carry: their symmetry and their
sign. André-Louis **Cholesky**, a French artillery officer and geodesist, found around
1910 — while solving the giant least-squares systems of triangulation surveys by hand —
that for these matrices you can do elimination in *half* the arithmetic by exploiting the
symmetry, and the answer comes out as $A = LL^\top$. His method was published
posthumously in 1924 (he was killed in 1918); it is now the default solver for every
positive-definite system on Earth.

So three independent demands — **simulate** correlated noise, **whiten** correlated data,
and **solve** positive-definite systems fast — all point at the same object: a square root
of a positive-definite matrix. We build it twice, because the three jobs do not all want
the *same* root.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why do we get to assume the matrix is positive definite? Isn't that a strong
restriction?"* It is exactly the right restriction, for the same reason $\sqrt{a}$ needs
$a \ge 0$. A real square root of a number requires the number to be nonnegative; a real
"square root" $A = BB^\top$ of a matrix forces $A$ to be **positive semidefinite**, because
for any $x$, $x^\top A x = x^\top B B^\top x = \|B^\top x\|^2 \ge 0$ — a sum of squares can
never be negative. So *only* positive-(semi)definite matrices can be squares in the first
place; the restriction is not a convenience, it is the precondition. And the matrices the
applications hand us — covariance matrices, Gram matrices $X^\top X$, positive-definite
Hessians — are positive definite by construction ([GEOM-18]). The interviewer wants to hear
that $A = BB^\top \Rightarrow A \succeq 0$ is forced, so positive-definiteness is *necessary*,
not assumed for convenience.
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Before any algebra, here is the picture. A positive-definite matrix $A$, seen through its
quadratic form $q(x) = x^\top A x$, is an **ellipsoidal bowl** ([GEOM-17]): its level sets
$\{x : x^\top A x = 1\}$ are ellipses (in 2-D) or ellipsoids (in higher dimensions),
stretched long along the low-curvature directions and squashed short along the
high-curvature ones. A square root of $A$ is a *map that builds that ellipsoid from a
sphere.*

Concretely: take the unit circle — the set of $z$ with $\|z\| = 1$. Apply a matrix $B$ to
every point, getting the set of all $Bz$. That image is an ellipse. **The ellipse you get
is the unit ellipse of $A = BB^\top$.** Different square roots $B$ trace the *same* final
ellipse by *different routes*:

- The **symmetric root** $A^{1/2}$ stretches the circle straight along the eigen-axes — a
  pure scaling in the natural directions of $A$, no rotation. It is the "honest" square
  root: it deforms the circle the minimal, symmetric way.
- The **Cholesky factor** $L$ reaches the same ellipse by a *shear* — it stretches the
  first axis, then skews the second relative to it. The end ellipse is identical, but $L$
  gets there by a triangular sequence of moves, which is exactly what makes it cheap to
  compute and to invert.

@fig-two-roots draws this: one ellipse, two factorizations, two different ways the circle
flows into it.

```{python}
#| label: fig-two-roots
#| fig-cap: "Two square roots of the same positive-definite matrix $A=\\begin{psmallmatrix}4&2\\\\2&3\\end{psmallmatrix}$, each mapping the unit circle (grey) to the *same* ellipse $\\{Bz:\\|z\\|=1\\}$ (which is the unit ellipse of $A$). **Left:** the symmetric root $A^{1/2}$ stretches the circle straight along the eigen-axes of $A$ (red, green) — a pure, rotation-free scaling. **Right:** the Cholesky factor $L$ reaches the identical ellipse by a triangular shear: stretch $x_1$, then skew $x_2$. Same destination, different route. The image of the standard axes (blue arrows) shows how each factor moves the coordinate frame."
A = np.array([[4.0, 2.0], [2.0, 3.0]])

# symmetric root via spectral theorem
w, Q = np.linalg.eigh(A)
Ahalf = Q @ np.diag(np.sqrt(w)) @ Q.T
# Cholesky factor (lower-triangular)
L = np.linalg.cholesky(A)

theta = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 200)
circle = np.vstack([np.cos(theta), np.sin(theta)])
e_sym = Ahalf @ circle
e_chol = L @ circle
I2 = np.eye(2)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.4, 4.6))
for ax, B, ttl, col in [(axL, Ahalf, "symmetric root $A^{1/2}$", ACCENT),
                        (axR, L, "Cholesky factor $L$", HILITE)]:
    ax.plot(circle[0], circle[1], color=GREY, lw=1.4, ls="--", label="unit circle")
    img = B @ circle
    ax.plot(img[0], img[1], color=col, lw=2.4, label="image $\\{Bz\\}$ = ellipse of $A$")
    # where the standard axes land
    for k in range(2):
        draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), tuple(B[:, k]), color=VEC, lw=2.2)
    # eigen-axes of A on the symmetric panel for reference
    if ax is axL:
        for k, c in zip(range(2), (HILITE, ACCENT)):
            draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), tuple(np.sqrt(w[k])*Q[:, k]), color=c, lw=1.6, ls=":")
    ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.set_xlim(-3.6, 3.6); ax.set_ylim(-3.6, 3.6)
    ax.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); ax.set_ylabel("$x_2$"); ax.set_title(ttl)
    ax.legend(fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9, loc="upper left")
plt.tight_layout()
```

Hold this image. Everything below is the careful version of "build the ellipse of $A$ from
a circle, either by symmetric stretching or by triangular shearing."

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

We need one fact about positive-definite matrices throughout, so we state it cleanly and
recall why it holds — it is owned by [GEOM-18], but a self-contained article cannot lean on
the word alone.

::: {#def-pd}
**Definition (positive definite).** A symmetric matrix $A$ is **positive definite** (written
$A \succ 0$) if $x^\top A x > 0$ for every nonzero vector $x$. It is **positive
semidefinite** ($A \succeq 0$) if $x^\top A x \ge 0$ for every $x$.
:::

The single equivalence we reuse — proved in [GEOM-18] via the quadratic-form classification
of [GEOM-17], and which we restate so the logic here is complete — is:

> **A symmetric matrix is positive definite if and only if all its eigenvalues are
> positive.** *(Reason, in one line: by the principal-axis form @eq-principal-recall below,
> $x^\top A x = \sum_i \lambda_i z_i^2$; this is $> 0$ for all $x \neq 0$ exactly when every
> $\lambda_i > 0$, since the $z_i^2$ are nonnegative and not all zero.)*

That principal-axis form, imported from [GEOM-17], is: writing $A = Q\Lambda Q^\top$ from
the spectral theorem and setting $z = Q^\top x$,

$$
x^\top A x = \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i z_i^2, \qquad z_i = q_i^\top x .
$$ {#eq-principal-recall}

We will use it, and the equivalence above, freely.

## The matrix square root from the spectral theorem {#sec-spectral-root}

The cleanest square root is the one the spectral theorem builds with almost no work, so we
take it first. Recall ([GEOM-16]) that a real symmetric $A$ factors as

$$
A = Q\Lambda Q^\top, \qquad Q^\top Q = QQ^\top = I, \qquad
\Lambda = \operatorname{diag}(\lambda_1, \dots, \lambda_n),
$$ {#eq-spectral}

with $Q$ orthogonal (its columns the orthonormal eigenvectors $q_i$) and the $\lambda_i$
the real eigenvalues. If $A$ is positive definite, every $\lambda_i > 0$, so each
$\sqrt{\lambda_i}$ is a real positive number. Define

$$
\Lambda^{1/2} = \operatorname{diag}\bigl(\sqrt{\lambda_1}, \dots, \sqrt{\lambda_n}\bigr),
\qquad
A^{1/2} := Q\,\Lambda^{1/2}\,Q^\top .
$$ {#eq-sym-root-def}

::: {#thm-sym-root}
**Theorem (symmetric square root).** Let $A$ be symmetric positive definite. Then
$A^{1/2}$ defined by @eq-sym-root-def is symmetric, positive definite, and satisfies
$A^{1/2}A^{1/2} = A$. It is the **unique** symmetric positive-definite matrix whose square
is $A$.
:::

::: {.proof}
*It squares to $A$.* Multiply $A^{1/2}$ by itself, inserting the definition and using
$Q^\top Q = I$ to collapse the middle:

$$
A^{1/2}A^{1/2}
= \bigl(Q\Lambda^{1/2}Q^\top\bigr)\bigl(Q\Lambda^{1/2}Q^\top\bigr)
= Q\Lambda^{1/2}\underbrace{(Q^\top Q)}_{=I}\Lambda^{1/2}Q^\top
= Q\,\Lambda^{1/2}\Lambda^{1/2}\,Q^\top .
$$

Now $\Lambda^{1/2}\Lambda^{1/2}$ is a product of two diagonal matrices, which multiplies
entrywise on the diagonal: its $i$-th entry is $\sqrt{\lambda_i}\cdot\sqrt{\lambda_i} =
\lambda_i$. So $\Lambda^{1/2}\Lambda^{1/2} = \Lambda$, and $A^{1/2}A^{1/2} = Q\Lambda Q^\top
= A$ by @eq-spectral.

*It is symmetric.* Transpose @eq-sym-root-def using $(MNP)^\top = P^\top N^\top M^\top$
([GEOM-10]), and that a diagonal matrix is its own transpose:
$(A^{1/2})^\top = (Q^\top)^\top (\Lambda^{1/2})^\top Q^\top = Q\Lambda^{1/2}Q^\top = A^{1/2}.$

*It is positive definite.* $A^{1/2}$ has the spectral form @eq-spectral with the *same*
eigenvectors $Q$ and eigenvalues $\sqrt{\lambda_i}$, each positive; by the equivalence in
@sec-formal, all-positive eigenvalues means positive definite.

*Uniqueness.* Suppose $B$ is also symmetric positive definite with $B^2 = A$. Since $B$ is
symmetric, the spectral theorem gives $B = P M P^\top$ with $P$ orthogonal and $M$ diagonal
with positive entries $\mu_j$ (positive because $B \succ 0$). Then $B^2 = PM^2P^\top$, and
this equals $A$. So $PM^2P^\top$ is a spectral decomposition of $A$: the columns of $P$ are
eigenvectors of $A$ with eigenvalues $\mu_j^2$. The eigenvalues of $A$ are uniquely the
$\lambda_i$, so the multiset $\{\mu_j^2\}$ equals $\{\lambda_i\}$, forcing $\mu_j =
\sqrt{\lambda_{(j)}}$ (positive root, since $\mu_j > 0$). Reordering so $P$ matches $Q$
eigenvector-by-eigenvector, $M = \Lambda^{1/2}$ and $B = Q\Lambda^{1/2}Q^\top = A^{1/2}$.
The matching of eigenvectors is exact on each eigenspace because on a repeated eigenvalue
*any* orthonormal basis is eigenvectors of both $A$ and $B$ with the forced value
$\sqrt{\lambda}$, so $B$ acts as $\sqrt{\lambda}\,I$ there, identically to $A^{1/2}$.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

A few consequences we will use. First, the **inverse square root** is immediate: since $A
= Q\Lambda Q^\top$ with positive $\lambda_i$, the inverse is $A^{-1} = Q\Lambda^{-1}Q^\top$
(invert the eigenvalues, keep the eigenvectors — orthogonal $Q$ makes inversion trivial,
[GEOM-16]), and its symmetric square root is

$$
A^{-1/2} = Q\,\Lambda^{-1/2}\,Q^\top,
\qquad \Lambda^{-1/2} = \operatorname{diag}\!\Bigl(\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda_1}},\dots,\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda_n}}\Bigr),
$$ {#eq-inv-root}

which satisfies $A^{-1/2}A^{-1/2} = A^{-1}$ and $A^{-1/2} = (A^{1/2})^{-1}$ by the identical
algebra. This is the **whitening** matrix of @sec-whitening. Second, the symmetric root
commutes with $A$: $A^{1/2}A = Q\Lambda^{1/2}Q^\top Q\Lambda Q^\top = Q\Lambda^{3/2}Q^\top =
AA^{1/2}$, because diagonal matrices commute. The symmetric root lives in the *same eigen-frame*
as $A$ — it never rotates, it only rescales each principal axis by $\sqrt{\lambda_i}$. That
is the rotation-free stretching of @fig-two-roots, made precise.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"How many square roots does a positive-definite matrix have?"* If you demand the root be
**symmetric positive definite**, exactly **one** — the theorem above. If you drop symmetry
and only ask for some $B$ with $BB^\top = A$, there are **infinitely many**: any $B = A^{1/2}R$
with $R$ orthogonal works, since $BB^\top = A^{1/2}RR^\top A^{1/2} = A^{1/2}A^{1/2} = A$
(using $RR^\top = I$). Geometrically, $R$ is a rotation you apply to the unit circle
*before* stretching — it changes the route but not the final ellipse. The symmetric root is
the $R = I$ choice; the Cholesky factor is the choice of $R$ that makes $B$ triangular. That
freedom is exactly why two genuinely different "square roots" can both be correct.
:::

## The Cholesky factorization, derived entry by entry {#sec-cholesky-derive}

Now the workhorse. We *demand* a factorization $A = LL^\top$ with $L$ lower-triangular, and
we simply solve for the entries of $L$ one at a time. The triangular shape is what makes
this both possible in closed form and cheap; let us watch it fall out.

A lower-triangular $L$ has $L_{ij} = 0$ for $j > i$ (zeros above the diagonal). Write out
the $(i,j)$ entry of the product $LL^\top$. By the definition of matrix multiplication, the
$(i,j)$ entry of $LL^\top$ is row $i$ of $L$ dotted with column $j$ of $L^\top$, and column
$j$ of $L^\top$ is row $j$ of $L$:

$$
(LL^\top)_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^n L_{ik}\,(L^\top)_{kj} = \sum_{k=1}^n L_{ik} L_{jk} .
$$

Because $L$ is lower-triangular, $L_{ik} = 0$ once $k > i$ and $L_{jk} = 0$ once $k > j$, so
the sum runs only up to $k = \min(i,j)$. Setting this equal to $A_{ij}$ gives the **defining
equations** of Cholesky:

$$
A_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{\min(i,j)} L_{ik} L_{jk} \qquad \text{for all } i \ge j .
$$ {#eq-chol-system}

(We need only $i \ge j$ — the lower triangle — because $A$ is symmetric, so the upper
triangle is determined automatically, and indeed $(LL^\top)$ is automatically symmetric.)
This is a triangular system in the unknowns $L_{ij}$, and we solve it by sweeping
**column by column, top to bottom**. The trick is to look at @eq-chol-system for a fixed
$(i,j)$ and peel off the last term of the sum.

**Diagonal entries ($i = j$).** Here $\min(i,j) = j$, so

$$
A_{jj} = \sum_{k=1}^{j} L_{jk}^2 = \underbrace{\sum_{k=1}^{j-1} L_{jk}^2}_{\text{already known}} + L_{jj}^2 .
$$

Solving for the one new unknown $L_{jj}$:

$$
\boxed{\;L_{jj} = \sqrt{\,A_{jj} - \sum_{k=1}^{j-1} L_{jk}^2\,}\;}
$$ {#eq-chol-diag}

**Off-diagonal entries ($i > j$).** Here $\min(i,j) = j$, so

$$
A_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{j} L_{ik} L_{jk} = \underbrace{\sum_{k=1}^{j-1} L_{ik} L_{jk}}_{\text{already known}} + L_{ij} L_{jj} .
$$

Solving for the new unknown $L_{ij}$ (dividing by $L_{jj}$, which the diagonal step just
computed):

$$
\boxed{\;L_{ij} = \frac{1}{L_{jj}}\Bigl(A_{ij} - \sum_{k=1}^{j-1} L_{ik} L_{jk}\Bigr)\;}
$$ {#eq-chol-offdiag}

These two boxed formulas **are** the Cholesky algorithm. Read the dependency structure:
@eq-chol-diag for $L_{jj}$ needs only entries $L_{jk}$ with $k < j$ (earlier columns of row
$j$); @eq-chol-offdiag for $L_{ij}$ needs $L_{jj}$ (just computed) and earlier-column
entries of rows $i$ and $j$. So if we process **column $j = 1, 2, \dots, n$ in order**, and
within each column do the diagonal entry first and then the entries below it, every quantity
on the right-hand side is already known. The factorization is forced: at each step exactly
one unknown is determined, so $L$ is **unique** (given the positive-root convention in
@eq-chol-diag). Let us see it run on a $3\times 3$ to dispel any mystery.

::: {#thm-chol-3x3 .unnumbered}
**Worked $3\times 3$.** Take
$$
A = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 2 & -2 \\ 2 & 10 & 2 \\ -2 & 2 & 5 \end{pmatrix}.
$$
**Column 1.** $L_{11} = \sqrt{A_{11}} = \sqrt{4} = 2$ (no earlier terms). Then $L_{21} =
A_{21}/L_{11} = 2/2 = 1$ and $L_{31} = A_{31}/L_{11} = -2/2 = -1$.
**Column 2.** $L_{22} = \sqrt{A_{22} - L_{21}^2} = \sqrt{10 - 1} = 3$. Then $L_{32} =
(A_{32} - L_{31}L_{21})/L_{22} = (2 - (-1)(1))/3 = 3/3 = 1$.
**Column 3.** $L_{33} = \sqrt{A_{33} - L_{31}^2 - L_{32}^2} = \sqrt{5 - 1 - 1} = \sqrt{3}$.
So
$$
L = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 3 & 0 \\ -1 & 1 & \sqrt{3} \end{pmatrix}.
$$
Multiplying $LL^\top$ back out confirms it. Row 1 of $L$ dotted with itself gives
$(LL^\top)_{11} = 2^2 = 4 = A_{11}$; row 2 with row 1 gives $(LL^\top)_{21} = 1\cdot2 = 2 = A_{21}$;
row 2 with itself gives $1^2 + 3^2 = 10 = A_{22}$; row 3 with row 1 gives $(-1)(2) = -2 = A_{31}$;
row 3 with row 2 gives $(-1)(1) + (1)(3) = 2 = A_{32}$; and row 3 with itself gives
$(-1)^2 + 1^2 + (\sqrt3)^2 = 1+1+3 = 5 = A_{33}$. Every entry of $A$ is recovered, so
$LL^\top = A$.
:::

```{python}
#| label: chol-3x3-check
#| echo: true
import numpy as np
A = np.array([[4., 2., -2.], [2., 10., 2.], [-2., 2., 5.]])
L = np.linalg.cholesky(A)            # SciPy/NumPy give the lower-triangular factor
print("L =\n", np.round(L, 4))
print("\nL L^T reconstructs A:", np.allclose(L @ L.T, A))
print("diagonal of L (must be > 0):", np.round(np.diag(L), 4))
```

The printout matches the hand computation: $L_{33} = \sqrt 3 \approx 1.732$, and $LL^\top$
returns $A$ exactly. Every step required only the four basic operations plus **one square
root per diagonal entry** — and that square root is where positive-definiteness enters.

## Existence and uniqueness: Cholesky works *iff* $A$ is positive definite {#sec-chol-exist}

The algorithm of @sec-cholesky-derive runs into exactly one possible obstruction: the
quantity under the square root in @eq-chol-diag,

$$
d_j := A_{jj} - \sum_{k=1}^{j-1} L_{jk}^2,
$$

must be **positive** — otherwise $L_{jj}$ is not a positive real and the recursion dies
(and even if $d_j = 0$, we cannot divide by $L_{jj} = 0$ in @eq-chol-offdiag). The
remarkable fact is that "$d_j > 0$ at every step" is not an extra hypothesis to check — it
is *equivalent* to $A$ being positive definite.

::: {#thm-chol-exist}
**Theorem (Cholesky existence and uniqueness).** A symmetric matrix $A$ has a factorization
$A = LL^\top$ with $L$ lower-triangular and **strictly positive diagonal** if and only if
$A$ is positive definite. When it exists, $L$ is unique.
:::

::: {.proof}
**($\Leftarrow$) If $A \succ 0$, the algorithm succeeds.** We show every pivot $d_j > 0$ by
induction on the column $j$, proving the stronger statement that after processing columns
$1, \dots, j$, the top-left $j\times j$ block of $A$ equals $L_{(j)}L_{(j)}^\top$, where
$L_{(j)}$ is the $j\times j$ lower-triangular block built so far, and $L_{(j)}$ has positive
diagonal.

*Base case $j = 1$:* $d_1 = A_{11} = e_1^\top A e_1 > 0$ since $A \succ 0$ (take $x = e_1$,
the first standard basis vector, in the definition $x^\top A x > 0$). So $L_{11} = \sqrt{d_1}$
is real and positive, and the $1\times 1$ block matches.

*Inductive step:* suppose columns $1, \dots, j-1$ are done, so the $(j-1)\times(j-1)$
leading block satisfies $A_{(j-1)} = L_{(j-1)}L_{(j-1)}^\top$ with $L_{(j-1)}$ having
positive diagonal — hence invertible (a triangular matrix is invertible iff its diagonal
has no zeros, since $\det L_{(j-1)} = \prod_{m<j} L_{mm} \neq 0$). We must show the next
pivot $d_j = A_{jj} - \sum_{k<j} L_{jk}^2$ is positive. The strategy: first build the block
identity at size $j$ *as an algebraic consequence of the formulas* (with $d_j$ still of
unknown sign), then use a determinant to read off that sign.

*Build the size-$j$ block identity.* Partition the leading $j\times j$ block of $A$ and the
candidate factor $L_{(j)}$ as
$$
A_{(j)} = \begin{psmallmatrix} A_{(j-1)} & a \\ a^\top & A_{jj} \end{psmallmatrix},
\qquad
L_{(j)} = \begin{psmallmatrix} L_{(j-1)} & 0 \\ \ell^\top & L_{jj} \end{psmallmatrix},
$$
where $a = (A_{1j}, \dots, A_{j-1,j})^\top$ is the part of column $j$ above the diagonal,
$\ell = (L_{j1}, \dots, L_{j,j-1})^\top$ is row $j$ of $L$ left of the diagonal (the entries
@eq-chol-offdiag produces), and for now we carry the diagonal slot through its *square*
$L_{jj}^2 := d_j$ — we have not yet established $d_j>0$, so we postpone taking the root.
Multiplying the blocks,
$$
L_{(j)}L_{(j)}^\top
= \begin{psmallmatrix} L_{(j-1)}L_{(j-1)}^\top & L_{(j-1)}\ell \\
\ell^\top L_{(j-1)}^\top & \ell^\top\ell + L_{jj}^2 \end{psmallmatrix}.
$$
The top-left block is $A_{(j-1)}$ by the inductive hypothesis. For the top-right block,
the $i$-th entry of $L_{(j-1)}\ell$ (for $i = 1,\dots,j-1$) is $\sum_{k=1}^{j-1} L_{ik}L_{jk}$.
Each $\ell_k = L_{jk}$ was produced by @eq-chol-offdiag applied to the entry $(j,k)$ with
$k<j$, and @eq-chol-offdiag is precisely the rearrangement of the defining equation
@eq-chol-system that enforces $\sum_{m=1}^{\min(i,j)} L_{im}L_{jm} = A_{ij}$. For $i<j$ that
sum runs to $m=i\le j-1$, and since $L_{im}=0$ for $m>i$ it equals $\sum_{k=1}^{j-1}L_{ik}L_{jk}$.
So the $i$-th entry of $L_{(j-1)}\ell$ is $A_{ij}=a_i$: the top-right block is $a$
(and the bottom-left block is its transpose). The bottom-right entry is
$\ell^\top\ell + L_{jj}^2 = \sum_{k<j}L_{jk}^2 + d_j = A_{jj}$ by the *definition* of $d_j$.
Hence $L_{(j)}L_{(j)}^\top = A_{(j)}$ holds **identically in $d_j$** — it is an algebraic
rearrangement of @eq-chol-offdiag and the definition of $d_j$, with no sign assumption yet.

*Now read off the sign.* Consider the $j \times j$ leading principal submatrix $A_{(j)}$.
*Every leading principal submatrix of a positive-definite matrix is positive definite*:
for any nonzero $y \in \mathbb{R}^j$, pad it with zeros to $\tilde y = (y, 0, \dots, 0)^\top
\in \mathbb{R}^n$; then $y^\top A_{(j)} y = \tilde y^\top A \tilde y > 0$ because the padding
zeros out exactly the rows and columns of $A$ outside the block. So $A_{(j)} \succ 0$, hence
**has positive determinant** (a positive-definite matrix has all eigenvalues
positive, so $\det = \prod \lambda_i > 0$).

Take determinants of the block identity $A_{(j)} = L_{(j)}L_{(j)}^\top$ just established,
using $\det(MN) = \det M \det N$, $\det(M^\top)=\det M$, and $\det L_{(j)} = \prod_{m\le j} L_{mm}$ (determinant
of a triangular matrix is the product of its diagonal, [GEOM-13]):

$$
\det A_{(j)} = \bigl(\det L_{(j)}\bigr)^2 = \Bigl(\prod_{m=1}^{j} L_{mm}\Bigr)^2
= \Bigl(\prod_{m=1}^{j-1} L_{mm}\Bigr)^2 \cdot d_j .
$$

The left side is positive (just shown), and the bracket on the right is positive by the
inductive hypothesis (earlier diagonals positive). Therefore $d_j = \det A_{(j)} \big/
\bigl(\prod_{m<j} L_{mm}\bigr)^2 > 0$. So $L_{jj} = \sqrt{d_j}$ is real and positive, the
recursion continues, and the block identity extends to size $j$. By induction it reaches $j = n$,
producing the full $A = LL^\top$ with positive diagonal.

**($\Rightarrow$) If such an $L$ exists, $A \succ 0$.** Suppose $A = LL^\top$ with $L$
lower-triangular and positive diagonal. For any $x \neq 0$,

$$
x^\top A x = x^\top L L^\top x = (L^\top x)^\top (L^\top x) = \|L^\top x\|^2 \ge 0,
$$

a sum of squares. Equality $\|L^\top x\|^2 = 0$ would force $L^\top x = 0$; but $L^\top$ is
upper-triangular with the same positive diagonal as $L$, hence invertible, so $L^\top x = 0$
implies $x = 0$. Thus for $x \neq 0$ we get $x^\top A x > 0$: $A$ is positive definite.

**Uniqueness** was established constructively in @sec-cholesky-derive: at each step exactly
one unknown is solved for, and the positive-root convention in @eq-chol-diag removes the
$\pm$ ambiguity. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This is the elegant heart of the article: **the algorithm is its own existence test.** You
do not check positive-definiteness first and then factor; you just *try* to factor, and the
factorization completes (all pivots positive) if and only if the matrix was positive
definite. A negative or zero pivot is a *certificate* that $A$ is not positive definite. We
exploit exactly this in @sec-edge.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What's the fastest way to test whether a symmetric matrix is positive definite?"*
**Attempt a Cholesky factorization and see if it succeeds** — i.e. whether every pivot
$d_j$ stays positive. This is the standard answer, and it is faster and more numerically
trustworthy than computing all eigenvalues or checking determinants of every leading minor
(Sylvester's criterion). In code it is `numpy.linalg.cholesky(A)` wrapped in a
`try/except LinAlgError`: an exception means "not positive definite." Eigenvalues cost more
and round-off can make a true zero eigenvalue look slightly negative; the Cholesky test
costs $\tfrac13 n^3$ flops and gives a clean yes/no with the factor as a bonus.
:::

## Why Cholesky is half the cost: the flop count {#sec-flops}

The "twice as fast" claim deserves its number. The dominant cost of @eq-chol-diag and
@eq-chol-offdiag is the inner sum $\sum_{k=1}^{j-1}$, each term a multiply–add. Summing the
work over all entries $i \ge j$:

$$
\text{multiply–adds} \approx \sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{i=j}^{n}\sum_{k=1}^{j-1} 1
= \sum_{j=1}^{n} (n - j + 1)(j-1) .
$$

Approximating the sum by an integral for large $n$ (replace $j$ by a continuous variable
running $0$ to $n$): $\int_0^n (n - j)\,j\,dj = \tfrac{n^3}{2} - \tfrac{n^3}{3} =
\tfrac{n^3}{6}$. Each multiply–add is two floating-point operations, so Cholesky costs
about $\tfrac{n^3}{3}$ flops. General **LU / Gaussian elimination** ([GEOM-11]) costs about
$\tfrac{2n^3}{3}$ flops. So Cholesky is **half** the arithmetic — precisely the saving
Cholesky himself found by hand, and the reason it is the default for positive-definite
systems. (It also needs no pivoting for stability: positive-definiteness guarantees the
pivots stay safely positive, another gift of the structure.)

# The three payoffs {#sec-payoffs}

We now cash in the factorization on the three jobs that motivated it, each carried through
on honest data. These are not separate tricks; they are the *same* square root, read three
ways.

## Payoff 1 — Sampling a correlated Gaussian: $x = Lz$ {#sec-sampling}

The simulation problem from @sec-why: we want random vectors with a prescribed covariance
$\Sigma$, but our random-number generator only produces **uncorrelated** standard
components — a vector $z$ whose covariance is the identity $I$. We need a matrix $B$ that
*installs* the correlations. The governing fact is the **transformation rule for
covariance**, which we derive here so it is not a black box.

Let $z$ be a random vector with mean $0$ and covariance $\operatorname{Cov}(z) =
\mathbb{E}[zz^\top] = I$ (uncorrelated unit-variance components). Form $x = Bz$ for a fixed
matrix $B$. Then $x$ has mean $\mathbb{E}[x] = B\,\mathbb{E}[z] = 0$, and its covariance is

$$
\operatorname{Cov}(x) = \mathbb{E}[xx^\top]
= \mathbb{E}[(Bz)(Bz)^\top]
= \mathbb{E}[B z z^\top B^\top]
= B\,\mathbb{E}[zz^\top]\,B^\top
= B I B^\top = BB^\top .
$$ {#eq-cov-transform}

(We pulled the constant matrices $B, B^\top$ outside the expectation — expectation is
linear — and used $\mathbb{E}[zz^\top] = I$.) So **the covariance of $Bz$ is $BB^\top$**.
To get covariance $\Sigma$, choose any $B$ with $BB^\top = \Sigma$: a square root. The
**Cholesky factor $L$** is the natural choice — it is triangular, cheap, and turns the
sampler into a single matrix-vector product:

$$
\boxed{\;x = Lz, \qquad L L^\top = \Sigma \;\Longrightarrow\; \operatorname{Cov}(x) = \Sigma.\;}
$$ {#eq-sampler}

@fig-sampling runs this: standard uncorrelated noise on the left, and on the right the same
noise after $x = Lz$, now wearing the target covariance ellipse. We verify the empirical
covariance matches $\Sigma$.

```{python}
#| label: fig-sampling
#| fig-cap: "Sampling a correlated Gaussian by $x = Lz$. **Left:** 2000 draws of uncorrelated standard noise $z$ (covariance $\\approx I$, a round cloud). **Right:** the same draws after $x = Lz$ with $L$ the Cholesky factor of the target $\\Sigma=\\begin{psmallmatrix}3&1.8\\\\1.8&2\\end{psmallmatrix}$. The cloud has acquired exactly the target covariance ellipse (red); the empirical covariance (printed) matches $\\Sigma$ to sampling error. The Cholesky factor *installs* the correlation structure."
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
Sigma = np.array([[3.0, 1.8], [1.8, 2.0]])
L = np.linalg.cholesky(Sigma)
z = rng.normal(size=(2, 2000))
x = L @ z

def cov_ellipse(ax, C, color, lw=2.4, nsig=2.0, label=None):
    w, V = np.linalg.eigh(C)
    tt = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 200)
    pts = V @ np.diag(nsig*np.sqrt(w)) @ np.vstack([np.cos(tt), np.sin(tt)])
    ax.plot(pts[0], pts[1], color=color, lw=lw, label=label)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.4, 4.4), sharex=True, sharey=True)
axL.scatter(z[0], z[1], s=6, color=PALE, alpha=0.5)
cov_ellipse(axL, np.eye(2), GREY, label="target cov $I$")
axL.set_title("uncorrelated $z$ (cov $\\approx I$)")
axL.set_aspect("equal"); axL.set_xlabel("$z_1$"); axL.set_ylabel("$z_2$")
axL.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)

axR.scatter(x[0], x[1], s=6, color=PALE, alpha=0.5)
cov_ellipse(axR, Sigma, HILITE, label="target cov $\\Sigma$")
axR.set_title("$x=Lz$ (cov $\\approx\\Sigma$)")
axR.set_aspect("equal"); axR.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); axR.set_ylabel("$x_2$")
axR.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()

print("target Sigma =\n", Sigma)
print("empirical cov of x =\n", np.round(np.cov(x), 3))
```

The empirical covariance lands on $\Sigma$ to within sampling noise — the sampler works.
Note we could equally have used the symmetric root $\Sigma^{1/2}$ (it also satisfies
$BB^\top = \Sigma$ since $\Sigma^{1/2}$ is symmetric, so $BB^\top = \Sigma^{1/2}\Sigma^{1/2}
= \Sigma$), but $L$ is the universal choice in practice: triangular means one cheap
matrix-vector product and no eigendecomposition. This is *the* method behind drawing from a
multivariate normal — it is literally what `numpy.random.multivariate_normal` does
internally (via a Cholesky of the covariance).

## Payoff 2 — Whitening: stripping correlation with $\Sigma^{-1/2}$ {#sec-whitening}

Sampling installed correlation; whitening *removes* it. Given data with covariance
$\Sigma$, we want to transform it so the new covariance is the identity — every direction
unit-variance and uncorrelated, a perfectly round cloud. By the transformation rule
@eq-cov-transform, applying a matrix $W$ to the data gives covariance $W\Sigma W^\top$, and
we want that to equal $I$:

$$
W \Sigma W^\top = I .
$$ {#eq-whiten-cond}

The **symmetric inverse root** $W = \Sigma^{-1/2}$ from @eq-inv-root does it. Check, using
that $\Sigma^{-1/2}$ is symmetric so $W^\top = W$, and $\Sigma^{-1/2}\Sigma\Sigma^{-1/2} =
\Sigma^{-1/2}\Sigma^{1/2}\Sigma^{1/2}\Sigma^{-1/2} = I$:

$$
W\Sigma W^\top = \Sigma^{-1/2}\,\Sigma\,\Sigma^{-1/2}
= \Sigma^{-1/2}\bigl(\Sigma^{1/2}\Sigma^{1/2}\bigr)\Sigma^{-1/2}
= \bigl(\Sigma^{-1/2}\Sigma^{1/2}\bigr)\bigl(\Sigma^{1/2}\Sigma^{-1/2}\bigr) = I .
$$

So $x \mapsto \Sigma^{-1/2}x$ whitens. This is the **symmetric** root's moment to shine:
because it is symmetric and lives in $\Sigma$'s own eigen-frame, $\Sigma^{-1/2}$ whitens
*without rotating the data away from its natural axes* — it stretches each principal axis to
unit length and stops, which is why this particular whitening is called **ZCA** (zero-phase)
and is the one that keeps the whitened data looking as much like the original as possible.

@fig-whitening shows a correlated cloud collapsing to a round one under $\Sigma^{-1/2}$, and
contrasts it with the Cholesky whitener $L^{-1}$ (also valid — $L^{-1}\Sigma L^{-\top} =
L^{-1}LL^\top L^{-\top} = I$ — but it rotates/shears the cloud, since $L$ is triangular not
symmetric).

```{python}
#| label: fig-whitening
#| fig-cap: "Whitening removes correlation. **Left:** a correlated data cloud with covariance $\\Sigma$ and its 2-$\\sigma$ ellipse. **Middle:** after the symmetric whitener $\\Sigma^{-1/2}$ — a round, unit-covariance cloud, with the data left in its natural orientation (no spurious rotation). **Right:** after the Cholesky whitener $L^{-1}$ — also perfectly white (round), but rotated/sheared, because the triangular factor reaches $I$ by a different route. Both achieve $\\mathrm{cov}=I$; the symmetric root is the rotation-free one."
rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
Sigma = np.array([[4.0, 2.6], [2.6, 2.5]])
L = np.linalg.cholesky(Sigma)
w, Q = np.linalg.eigh(Sigma)
Sig_inv_half = Q @ np.diag(1/np.sqrt(w)) @ Q.T
data = (L @ rng.normal(size=(2, 1500)))      # correlated, cov = Sigma
white_sym = Sig_inv_half @ data
white_chol = np.linalg.solve(L, data)        # = L^{-1} data

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(11.0, 3.8))
panels = [(data, Sigma, "raw: cov $=\\Sigma$", PALE, HILITE),
          (white_sym, np.eye(2), "$\\Sigma^{-1/2}$ whitened", PALE, ACCENT),
          (white_chol, np.eye(2), "$L^{-1}$ whitened", PALE, VEC)]
for ax, (d, C, ttl, pc, ec) in zip(axes, panels):
    ax.scatter(d[0], d[1], s=6, color=pc, alpha=0.55)
    cov_ellipse(ax, np.cov(d), ec, label="empirical 2$\\sigma$")
    ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.set_xlim(-7, 7); ax.set_ylim(-7, 7)
    ax.set_title(ttl); ax.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); ax.set_ylabel("$x_2$")
    ax.legend(fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9, loc="upper left")
plt.tight_layout()

print("cov after symmetric whitening:\n", np.round(np.cov(white_sym), 3))
print("cov after Cholesky whitening:\n", np.round(np.cov(white_chol), 3))
```

Both whitened clouds have covariance $\approx I$ (printed), confirming both factors work;
the visual difference is the orientation, exactly the "same ellipse, different route" of
@fig-two-roots run in reverse. **This is the algebraic engine of generalized least
squares** ([REGR-11]): when regression errors are correlated with covariance $\Sigma$,
multiplying the whole model $y = X\beta + \varepsilon$ by $\Sigma^{-1/2}$ produces a new
model whose errors are white, so ordinary least squares applies to the transformed
problem. We hand Regression the algebra; it supplies the statistics.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Cholesky or the symmetric root for whitening — does it matter which?"* For making the
covariance equal to $I$, **no** — both work, as @fig-whitening shows. It matters when you
care about *what the whitened coordinates mean*. The symmetric root $\Sigma^{-1/2}$ keeps
the data aligned with its original axes (ZCA whitening, used in image processing precisely
so whitened images still look like images). The Cholesky whitener $L^{-1}$ imposes an
*order* on the variables — it whitens the first variable, then the second given the first,
and so on — which is exactly right when the variables have a natural sequence (a time
series, a causal ordering) and is far cheaper. So: symmetric for geometric neutrality,
Cholesky for speed and for ordered/conditional structure.
:::

## Payoff 3 — Solving $Ax = b$ by forward and back substitution {#sec-solving}

The third payoff is the one Cholesky invented the factorization for. To solve $Ax = b$ with
$A = LL^\top$ positive definite, substitute the factorization:

$$
A x = b \;\Longleftrightarrow\; L\underbrace{L^\top x}_{=:\,y} = b .
$$

This splits one hard solve into **two easy triangular solves**, done in sequence:

1. **Forward substitution:** solve $Ly = b$ for $y$. Because $L$ is lower-triangular, the
   first equation involves only $y_1$ (so $y_1 = b_1/L_{11}$), the second only $y_1, y_2$
   (so $y_2 = (b_2 - L_{21}y_1)/L_{22}$), and so on down — each $y_i$ from already-known ones.
2. **Back substitution:** solve $L^\top x = y$ for $x$. Because $L^\top$ is
   upper-triangular, the *last* equation involves only $x_n$, the second-to-last only
   $x_{n-1}, x_n$, and so on **up** — each $x_i$ from already-known ones.

Each triangular solve costs only $\sim n^2$ operations (no inversion, no further
factoring); the one-time $\tfrac13 n^3$ Cholesky factorization is the only cubic cost, and
it is reused for *every* right-hand side $b$. We run a $3\times 3$ in full, then verify
against a direct solve.

```{python}
#| label: chol-solve
#| echo: true
import numpy as np

def forward_sub(L, b):
    n = len(b); y = np.zeros(n)
    for i in range(n):
        y[i] = (b[i] - L[i, :i] @ y[:i]) / L[i, i]
    return y

def back_sub(U, y):
    n = len(y); x = np.zeros(n)
    for i in reversed(range(n)):
        x[i] = (y[i] - U[i, i+1:] @ x[i+1:]) / U[i, i]
    return x

A = np.array([[4., 2., -2.], [2., 10., 2.], [-2., 2., 5.]])
b = np.array([4., 14., 5.])
L = np.linalg.cholesky(A)
y = forward_sub(L, b)        # solve L y = b
x = back_sub(L.T, y)         # solve L^T x = y
print("y (forward) =", np.round(y, 4))
print("x (back)    =", np.round(x, 4))
print("matches np.linalg.solve:", np.allclose(x, np.linalg.solve(A, b)))
```

The two-stage triangular solve reproduces the direct answer exactly. **Never invert $A$**:
forming $A^{-1}$ costs more, is less accurate, and — when all you want is $x$ — solves a
harder problem than you have. The practitioner reflex "*solve, don't invert*" ([GEOM-26])
is concretely *this*: factor once with Cholesky, then forward/back-substitute.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Do not Cholesky-factor a matrix that is only positive *semi*definite or merely
symmetric.** If $A$ has a zero eigenvalue (semidefinite), some pivot $d_j$ is zero and the
division in @eq-chol-offdiag blows up; if $A$ has a negative eigenvalue (indefinite), some
$d_j < 0$ and @eq-chol-diag takes the square root of a negative number. In floating point
the failure can be *silent and late*: round-off may keep early pivots barely positive and
only later produce a tiny-negative pivot, returning an `L` that does **not** satisfy $LL^\top
= A$. Always either (a) know $A \succ 0$ by construction, or (b) treat a Cholesky failure as
the diagnosis "not positive definite" rather than a bug to patch. For genuinely semidefinite
or indefinite matrices, reach for the spectral decomposition or the SVD ([GEOM-20]) instead,
which never divide by a pivot.
:::

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-edge}

The Core promised a factorization for positive-definite matrices; honesty requires showing
where positive-definiteness frays and the methods strain or break.

## The edge: near-singular covariance and the pivot collapse {#sec-edge-near}

Real covariance matrices drift toward the boundary of positive-definiteness all the time:
two features that are nearly redundant (a near-linear dependence) make $\Sigma$ nearly
**singular** — one eigenvalue close to zero. The matrix is still technically positive
definite, so Cholesky *succeeds*, but the last pivot $d_n$ is tiny, $L_{nn} = \sqrt{d_n}$ is
tiny, and dividing by it in subsequent steps amplifies round-off. This is the
ill-conditioned regime: the unit ellipse of $\Sigma$ is a long thin cigar ([GEOM-17]), and
both the sampler and the whitener become unstable because they must stretch or crush by a
near-infinite ratio. @fig-edge tracks the smallest pivot as a covariance is pushed toward
singular.

```{python}
#| label: fig-edge
#| fig-cap: "The edge of applicability. We build $\\Sigma_\\rho=\\begin{psmallmatrix}1&\\rho\\\\\\rho&1\\end{psmallmatrix}$ and push the correlation $\\rho\\to 1$, where the two features become collinear and $\\Sigma$ approaches singular. **Left:** the smallest Cholesky pivot $d_2 = 1-\\rho^2$ collapses to zero — the factorization survives but barely, $L_{22}=\\sqrt{1-\\rho^2}\\to 0$. **Right:** the resulting covariance ellipse degenerates from a circle ($\\rho=0$) to a near-line ($\\rho=0.99$): the cloud loses a dimension, whitening must divide by $\\sqrt{1-\\rho^2}\\to0$, and the problem is numerically lost before it is mathematically lost."
rhos = np.linspace(0, 0.999, 200)
pivots = 1 - rhos**2                  # d_2 for the 2x2 correlation matrix

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.6, 4.2))
axL.plot(rhos, pivots, color=HILITE)
axL.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=1.0)
axL.set_xlabel("correlation $\\rho$"); axL.set_ylabel("smallest pivot $d_2 = 1-\\rho^2$")
axL.set_title("Pivot collapses as $\\rho\\to1$")

tt = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 200)
circ = np.vstack([np.cos(tt), np.sin(tt)])
for rho, col in [(0.0, ACCENT), (0.8, VEC), (0.97, HILITE)]:
    S = np.array([[1.0, rho], [rho, 1.0]])
    Lr = np.linalg.cholesky(S)
    e = Lr @ circ
    axR.plot(e[0], e[1], color=col, lw=2.0, label=f"$\\rho={rho}$")
axR.set_aspect("equal"); axR.set_xlim(-2.2, 2.2); axR.set_ylim(-2.2, 2.2)
axR.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); axR.set_ylabel("$x_2$")
axR.set_title("Covariance ellipse degenerates to a line")
axR.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9, loc="upper left")
plt.tight_layout()
```

The fix when you hit this edge: **regularize** by adding a small positive multiple of the
identity, $\Sigma + \epsilon I$, which lifts every eigenvalue by $\epsilon$ (since
$(\Sigma + \epsilon I)$ has eigenvalues $\lambda_i + \epsilon$) and pulls the matrix safely
back into the well-conditioned interior — this is exactly **ridge regularization**
([REGR-14]) wearing its linear-algebra clothes, and the "jitter" routinely added to the
covariance of a Gaussian process before Cholesky. The honest statement: Cholesky does not
fail gracefully near the boundary; you must keep the matrix away from it.

## When Cholesky is the *wrong* tool {#sec-wrong-tool}

A clean applicability call, the kind an interview expects:

- **The matrix is not (even nearly) positive definite.** Indefinite or semidefinite — e.g.
  a saddle-shaped Hessian at a non-minimum, or a rank-deficient Gram matrix. Cholesky's
  square roots go imaginary or its pivots vanish. **Reach for** the eigendecomposition
  ([GEOM-16]) or the **SVD** ([GEOM-20]), which exist for *every* matrix and never divide
  by a pivot.
- **You need the actual eigenvalues / principal axes**, not just a factor — e.g. for PCA,
  for the condition number, for the directions of maximum variance. Cholesky's $L$ is *not*
  the eigenstructure; its diagonal entries are pivots, **not eigenvalues** (a common
  confusion). **Reach for** the spectral decomposition or SVD.
- **The matrix is rectangular** ($m \times n$, $m \neq n$). Cholesky is for square
  symmetric matrices only. To "square-root" a data matrix $X$ you instead factor the
  symmetric $X^\top X$ (which Cholesky *can* handle if it is positive definite), or — better
  — take the SVD of $X$ directly, which is the numerically sound route and avoids forming
  $X^\top X$ at all ([GEOM-26] explains why squaring the matrix squares the condition
  number).

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Cholesky versus the symmetric square root — when do you use each?"* Recite the trade:
**Cholesky** ($A = LL^\top$, triangular) is the **fast, default** choice for *solving*
positive-definite systems, for *sampling* a Gaussian ($x = Lz$), and for conditional /
ordered whitening — half the flops of LU, no eigendecomposition, no pivoting. The
**symmetric root** ($A^{1/2} = Q\Lambda^{1/2}Q^\top$) is the choice when you need the result
to *itself be symmetric* or geometrically neutral: rotation-free (ZCA) whitening, defining
the matrix exponential/logarithm, interpolating between covariances, and anywhere $A^{1/2}$
appears inside further symmetric algebra. The symmetric root costs an eigendecomposition
($\sim n^3$ with a larger constant) and is slower; you pay for it only when symmetry
matters. Default to Cholesky; upgrade to the symmetric root for symmetry or
interpretability.
:::

## A non-uniqueness trap {#sec-trap}

Finally, the conceptual edge from @sec-spectral-root, restated as a warning. "The square
root of $A$" is ambiguous unless you say *which* root. The phrase usually means the
symmetric one (unique), but `L` from Cholesky is *also* a square root in the
$BB^\top = A$ sense, and they are different matrices. Worse, some software returns the
**upper**-triangular Cholesky factor $R = L^\top$ with $A = R^\top R$ (the convention
matching $A = X^\top X$ from QR, [GEOM-07]) rather than the lower factor. Always check which
convention a library uses — `numpy.linalg.cholesky` returns **lower** $L$ with $A = LL^\top$;
`scipy.linalg.cholesky` defaults to **upper** $R$ with $A = R^\top R$. Mixing them transposes
your factor and silently corrupts a sampler or a solve.

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[GEOM-18 *Positive-Definite Matrices*]**, which characterized the "good"
matrices — $x^\top A x > 0$, all eigenvalues positive — and promised that "a Cholesky factor
exists" is yet another equivalent test. This article delivered that promise (@thm-chol-exist)
and built *both* square roots, the triangular and the symmetric, with their three payoffs.
It uses the spectral theorem ([GEOM-16]) for the symmetric root, the quadratic-form
classification ([GEOM-17]) for the positive-definite reasoning, and triangular solving
([GEOM-11]) for the application. From here the trunk continues:

- **[GEOM-20 *The Singular Value Decomposition*]** — the master factorization that works for
  *every* matrix, square or not, definite or not, which is exactly what you reach for when
  Cholesky's positive-definiteness requirement fails. For a symmetric positive-definite $A$
  the SVD $A = U\Sigma V^\top$ reduces to the spectral decomposition $A = Q\Lambda Q^\top$ of
  this article (one takes $U = V = Q$ and singular values $\sigma_i = \lambda_i$, since the
  eigenvalues are already positive) — so the spectral square root and the SVD-based root are
  the same matrix there; [GEOM-20] proves the general construction.
- **[GEOM-26 *Condition Number and Numerical Stability*]** — formalizes the edge of
  @sec-edge-near: why a tiny smallest pivot signals an ill-conditioned matrix, why "solve,
  don't invert" is the right reflex, and why Cholesky on the normal equations $X^\top X$ can
  *square* the condition number (so QR/SVD sometimes beat it).

And the cross-track hand-offs this article discharges:

- **[REGR-11 *Generalized Least Squares*]** — the whitening transform $\Sigma^{-1/2}$
  (@sec-whitening) is GLS's engine: pre-multiply a correlated-error model by $\Sigma^{-1/2}$
  to make ordinary least squares apply.
- **Probability / Inference** — the sampler $x = Lz$ (@sec-sampling) is how every correlated
  multivariate Gaussian is drawn; the covariance-transformation rule @eq-cov-transform is
  the bridge.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The original.** André-Louis Cholesky's method was published posthumously by Commandant
  Benoît as "Note sur une méthode de résolution des équations normales…," *Bulletin
  géodésique* 2 (1924) — written to solve the least-squares normal equations of geodetic
  triangulation by hand. The factorization predates the computer by decades and was designed
  for human computers exploiting symmetry to halve the labor.
- **The standard reference.** Golub & Van Loan, *Matrix Computations* — Chapter 4 treats
  Cholesky, its flop count, stability, and the banded/sparse variants (where Cholesky's
  preservation of structure makes it indispensable for large sparse positive-definite
  systems).
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *What if $A$ is symmetric but
  indefinite?* — there is an $LDL^\top$ factorization with a block-diagonal $D$ (the
  Bunch–Kaufman variant) that avoids square roots entirely; it generalizes Cholesky past the
  positive-definite boundary. *What is the "right" square root for matrices that are not even
  symmetric?* — the polar decomposition and the SVD ([GEOM-20]) answer that. *How do we keep
  a covariance estimate positive definite when data are scarce?* — shrinkage and
  regularization, a Statistical-Inference concern that consumes the $\Sigma + \epsilon I$
  idea of @sec-edge-near.
